coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot powders for photocatalytic reduction of quinones
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Qing
Song, Zhenchao
Xue, Chaorui
Li, Ning
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot powders without combining with any nanostructures or nanomaterials exhibit broadband light-absorption ability and they are proved to possess excellent photoreduction performance. Not only do they exhibit highly efficient reduction of quinones under an atmosphere of air and visible light irradiation, but they can be also used repeatedly without incurring obvious changes in photocatalytic activities. Our experimental evidences indicate that photoinduced electron transfer properties of carbon dots endow photoreducing capacities of their powders.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of coal pitch-based CDs, the inset is their EDS spectrum; (b) HRTEM image of coal pitch-based CDs; (c) XPS O1s spectra of coal pitch-based CDs and coal pitch; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of the powders of coal pitch and CDs.

(a) Schematic transformation of TBBQ and BQ into TBHQ and HQ, respectively; (b) UV spectra of the remaining TBBQ and its reduction product, TBHQ as a result of visible light irradiation in the presence of sample; (c) UV spectra of the remaining BQ and its reduction product, HQ as a result of visible light irradiation in the presence of sample; (d) Conversion rates of TBBQ and BQ after 10 min irradiation; (e) The recyclability of sample.

(a) PL spectra (480 nm excitation) of CDs without and with the different concentrations of TBBQ; (b) The corresponding PL decays of CDs without and with the different concentrations of TBBQ; (c) Schematic mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of TBBQ to TBHQ by CD powders.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot powders for photocatalytic reduction of quinones

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot powders without combining with any nanostructures or nanomaterials exhibit broadband light-absorption ability and they are proved to possess excellent photoreduction performance.

Carbon dot powders without combining with any nanostructures or nanomaterials exhibit broadband light-absorption ability and they are proved to possess excellent photoreduction performance. Not only do they exhibit highly efficient reduction of quinones under an atmosphere of air and visible light irradiation, but they can be also used repeatedly without incurring obvious changes in photocatalytic activities. Our experimental evidences indicate that photoinduced electron transfer properties of carbon dots endow photoreducing capacities of their powders.

ce:section.ce:para: As a promising photocatalyst, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great interest because of their low cost, widely available precursors, eco-friendly preparation, high stability, and tunable light-harvesting range [1–3]. At present, most of CD-based photocatalysts are the heterostructures by combining CDs with other nanomaterials [1,4]. If the band alignment between CDs and theircounterpart is appropriate, the CD-based heterostructure not only promotes separation of photogenerated charges but facilitates their transfer at interface [4]. Accordingly, the different CD-based heterostructures were fabricated and exhibited different activities in H2 evolution [5], CO2 conversion [6], organic synthesis [7] and dye degradation [8].

It is known that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of CDs depend on a number of factors, including their size, composition and shape [4]. Phenomenally, the interaction among CDs can incur self-quenching or the variation of recombination paths through either resonance energy transfer or electron transfer processes [2,4]. Consequently, most of researches attempted to conquer self-quenching resulting from aggregation by embedding CDs in solid matrices such as silica xerogel, polymers [9–12]. However, few reports take advantage of the undesirable aggregation toprovideinputon oneof CD applications. If the photogenerated electrons and holes in CDs are preferentially captured by thereactants on their surface rather than themselves, the resonance energy transfer or electron transfer causes recombination processes to terminate prematurely. Thus, the photogenerated charges will transfer to the photoactive reactant, inducing redox under the light irradiation.

Recently, we reported a method for the scalable preparation of CDs using coal pitch as carbon source [13]. As-prepared CDs showed concentration-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties, suggesting the presence of electron transfer between CDs. In this work, we first demonstrate that the aggregation of coal pitch-based CDs possess a strong visible absorption band and high photoreduction ability. The presented results indicate that the photoexcited electrons in CDs can be captured preferentially by quinone molecules on the migration process and thereby give rise to quinone reduction.

The preparation method of coal pitch-based CDs was reported in Ref. [13], and experimental details were also given in supporting information. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra from the as-prepared high concentration CD solution (2.3 g/L) display a strongest peak at c.a. 600 nm (see Fig. S1). Fig. 1 a shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the prepared CDs. High density CDs with uniform sizes are clearly observed. Further high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image indicates that the nanoparticles reveal crystalline features and they are close to one another (Fig. 1b). Moreover, there is nothing but C and O elements in nanoparticles based on the spectrum of energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, the inset of Fig. 1a). The types of carbon-oxygen bonds were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1c, XPS O 1s signal reveals the presence of both CO and CO bonds in CDs [14]. The carbonyl-group content in CDs reaches 56.93% and the ratio of CO bond to CO bond exceeds 1.32 according to XPS analysis. By contrast, the CO bonds are dominated in raw coal pitch. Thereby, the higher surface oxidation of the CDs could induce more surface defects and create more energy levels, giving rise to new optical properties and active sites of catalysis [14]. Fig. 1d displays the UV–vis spectra of the CD powders and solid coal pitch. There are clearly different in optical absorption. Coal pitch powder has uniform light absorption from UV to entire visible spectrum, but CD powder exhibits linear increase for wavelength from 800 to 450 nm besides stronger light absorption than coal pitch. This suggests that CD powder is in favor of solar energy capture.

Phenols are a huge class of compounds endowed with rich and fascinating chemistry and have been widely applied in the field of synthetic organic chemistry. Hydroquinone (HQ) and tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) are common derivatives of phenols and they are commonly synthesized by the reduction of counterpart quinones. Typically, the photocatalytic activities of CD powders were estimated through reduction of 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) and tert-butyl-1,4-benzoquinone (TBBQ) to HQ and TBHQ as shown in Fig. 2 a, respectively.

In order to prevent the signals from the CD powders from interfering with those of the photoactive reactants and products during the UV–vis absorbance measurements, 1 mg of sample powder was blended with approximately 10 μL of a polyvinyl butyral (PVB, 1 mg) ethanol (1 mL) solution under stirring. The resultant mixture was drop cast onto a glass sheet and then air-dried. The glass sheet coated with CD powders was used for photocatalytic performance test (See see Fig. S2).

Fig. 2b displays UV spectra of the remaining TBBQ and its reduction product, TBHQ as a result of visible light irradiation in the presence of sample. The absorption peaks at 290 and 252 nm are attributed to the presence of TBHQ and TBBQ, respectively [15]. From Fig. 2b, the absorbance at 252 nm decreases with a corresponding increase in absorbance at 290 nm. This implies that more TBBQ molecules are gradually reduced to TBHQ with irradiation time. Similarly, the transformation of BQ into HQ was also detected by UV–vis spectrometer on the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 2c, the typical absorbance of BQ at 245 nm decreases with a corresponding increase in absorbance of HQ at 288 nm under visible light irradiation. The conversion rates of BQ and TBBQ to HQ and TBHQ are ca. 62% and 78% after 10 min irradiation, respectively (Fig. 2d, the calculated method was provided in supporting information, Fig. S3). Taking the reduction of TBBQ as a sample, the photocatalytic activities of the sample remain stable during 8 sequential cycles (Fig. 2e).

Photoluminescence (PL) characterization is widely employed to investigate the charge transfer process in a photocatalyst. From Fig. 3 a, the luminescence intensities of coal pitch-based CDs in aqueous or organic solutions are quenched with the addition of TBBQ. Moreover, the corresponding luminescence decays can be deconvoluted with the use of a bi-exponential function and the resultant average lifetimes are 5.584, 5.073, 4.297, 4.069 ns when adding with 0, 10, 20 and 30 mM of TBBQ, respectively. All above results highlight that the electron transport from CDs to TBBQ molecules is faster than the process of radiative recombination at surface energy traps. This could be attributed to CO bond-terminated groups on the surface of CDs. For instance, Kladnik et al. demonstrated that the interface with CO bonds possessed ultrafast charge transport [16]. Our previous works also offered the evidences that the CO bonds can be an effective pathway to transfer photoinduced electron to catalytic reaction [14].

As shown in Fig. 3c, the reduction of TBBQ could be accomplished by photoirradiating CDs owing to their electron donating capabilities. The HOMO electrons in CDs were photoexcited to LUMO levels. Subsequently, such excited electrons on LUMO of CDs migrated to TBBQ molecules on the surface, producing TBBQ radical (TBBQ*¯). Finally, the highly reactive TBBQ*¯ was preferable to react with H+ in aqueous solution and resulted in the formation of TBHQ.

CD powders with broadband light-absorption ability were demonstrated to possess excellent photoreduction performance. Under an atmosphere of air, not only did CD powders exhibit highly efficient reduction of quinones at visible light irradiation, but they were able to use repeatedly without incurring obvious changes in photocatalytic activities. Their photoreduction performance resulted from the ultrafast electron transfer from CDs to the reactants due to the presence of CO bonds at CD surface. Therefore, CD powder alone can be applied in photocatalysis.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51272301, 51502270), Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, and North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars and Scientific Innovation Team.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2018.01.167.
